topic: A coach has to cut a player from the team
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the locker room hummed, a grating sound that amplified every nervous tick in Leo’s chest. He meticulously folded his jersey, the fabric feeling suddenly too rough against his skin. Coach Miller stood by the whiteboard, his arms crossed, the silence stretching between them like a taut wire. Leo kept his gaze fixed on the scuff marks on the floor, focusing intensely on the pattern, anything but meeting the coach’s eyes. When Coach Miller finally spoke, his voice was level, devoid of warmth. "Leo, we've had to make some difficult decisions about the roster moving forward." Leo swallowed hard, the dryness in his throat making the motion visible. He could feel the heat rising in his cheeks, a flush spreading from his neck up to his hairline. Every muscle in his jaw felt tight, a physical manifestation of the knot tightening in his stomach. He just wanted the floor to swallow him whole.

[story 2]

"Look, I know this isn't what you wanted to hear," Coach Davies began, leaning against the doorframe, his expression unreadable. Mark shifted his weight from one foot to the other, his hands fiddling incessantly with the drawstring of his sweatpants. The air in the room suddenly felt too thin, too heavy. He kept glancing at the door, anticipating the moment the conversation would end. When the coach finally delivered the news—that he wouldn't be making the final cut—Mark felt a strange numbness wash over him. His hands, which had been clenched into fists, slowly uncurled, hanging limp at his sides. He managed a weak nod, his mouth feeling dry and oddly slack. He wanted to look away, to melt into the concrete wall behind him, because the scrutiny of the room felt like a physical weight pressing down on his shoulders.

[story 3]

"This is tough, son. We need to be realistic about where we can place everyone," Coach Reynolds stated, tapping a pen against a stack of papers. Ethan stood rigidly by the water fountain, his posture unnaturally straight. He could hear the distant chatter of the other players, their easy camaraderie sounding like a mocking soundtrack to his isolation. When Coach Reynolds pointed toward the exit, indicating that Ethan would be taking a step back, Ethan’s breath hitched. He avoided looking at the coach, focusing instead on the condensation dripping down the cool metal of the water dispenser. A tremor ran through his hands, making him grip the edge of the counter until his knuckles were white. He felt every single eye in the room tracking his retreat, a thousand tiny beams of judgment boring into his back.
